title: Polymer clay faux-stone cabochons
subtitle: Three small dome-shaped cabochons in faux granite, marble, and turquoise
excerpt: A set of three small polymer clay cabochons in faux granite, marble, and turquoise patterns. Each cabochon is built by blending two or three colours of polymer clay, shaping into a low dome, and baking rigid. The cured cabochons are jewellery-blank-ready for ring settings or pendant bezels. Decorative only; not food-safe.
sourceNotes: Faux-stone colour blending in polymer clay is a contemporary studio technique developed by artists across the 1980s and 1990s. Technique derived from first principles; geological-stone reference from open-source geology image collections.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only; not food-safe even after curing.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven. The clay goes in soft and comes out rigid.
A polished gem or bead cut in a domed shape with a flat back, traditionally in stone but here in polymer clay. Sits into a ring or pendant bezel.

content.text: A
cabochon
is a small domed shape with a flat back, traditionally a polished gemstone set into a ring or pendant bezel. The faux-stone version uses
polymer clay

in two or three colours blended in different ways to read as granite, marble, or turquoise. The clay bakes hard in a domestic oven at 110°C and the cured cabochons drop into commercial jewellery findings. Polymer clay is decorative only and not food-safe.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven tray. Verify oven temperature with an oven thermometer before baking; many domestic ovens run hot or cold. Do not share the tray with food cookware.

content.text: Conditioning the clay

Polymer clay needs to be warmed and worked in the hands until it bends without cracking before it can be shaped. Squeeze and roll each colour separately for one or two minutes. The clay should soften enough that a rolled snake bends in a U without breaking. Cold straight-from-the-pack clay crumbles; conditioned clay holds detail.
Faux granite cabochon

Take 30 g of mid-grey clay and 10 g of black. Roll the grey into a flat sheet 5 mm thick. Pinch off small flecks of black (around 1 to 2 mm across) and press them randomly across the grey sheet. Fold the sheet in half and roll it back out, folding and rolling four or five times until the black flecks distribute evenly through the grey like granite chips. Don't overwork; the goal is small distinct flecks, not a uniform dark grey. Pinch off 8 g of the blended clay and roll into a small ball. Press the ball gently flat on the work surface until it forms a low dome around 18 mm across and 8 mm tall, with a flat back. Smooth the dome surface with the side of a finger.
Faux marble cabochon

Take 30 g of white clay and a 1 g pinch of dark brown or dark grey. Roll the white into a snake 10 mm thick and 8 cm long. Roll the dark colour into a thinner snake (3 mm thick and 6 cm long). Lay the dark snake on top of the white, twist the two together gently like a rope, then roll the rope back into a single snake. Fold the snake in half and twist again. Repeat once or twice. The dark clay should now run through the white in irregular vein patterns. Don't over-blend or the veins muddy into grey. Pinch off 8 g and shape into a low dome 18 mm across and 8 mm tall. Smooth the surface with a finger; the marble veins should still read clearly.
Faux turquoise cabochon

Take 30 g of pale turquoise clay and a 1 g pinch of dark brown. Pinch the brown into very small flecks (1 mm) and press them sparingly across the surface of a flattened turquoise sheet. Crumble the sheet between the fingers without fully blending, then press the crumbles back together into a small ball. The brown flecks scatter through the turquoise as matrix lines (the dark spider-web markings characteristic of natural turquoise). Pinch off 8 g and shape into a low dome 18 mm across and 8 mm tall. Smooth gently with a finger; press too hard and the matrix flattens out.
Baking

Place the three cabochons flat-back-down on a sheet of greaseproof paper on the baking tray, with at least 3 cm between each. Check the oven temperature with the oven thermometer: aim for 110°C (fan) and verify the thermometer reads the same. Bake for 25 minutes.
Curing

sets the clay rigid. Underbaked clay stays soft and breaks; overbaked clay browns and goes brittle. The temperature is what matters most: a 10°C overshoot for the full bake time turns pale colours brown.
Finishing

Let the cabochons cool on the tray for ten minutes before handling: they harden fully on cooling. Sand the flat back with 600-grit wet-and-dry sandpaper if it is uneven. Polish the dome surface by rubbing it gently with a soft cloth, then a paper towel. A drop of beeswax polish on the cloth deepens the finish. For a higher gloss, brush on a thin coat of acrylic gloss varnish. Set each cabochon into a commercial bezel ring or pendant setting using two-part epoxy glue.
Care

Polymer clay is not food-safe. Cabochons set into jewellery are fine for normal daytime wear. Avoid hand-cream contact: oils and acetone can soften the clay's surface over time. Wipe clean with a soft dry cloth. Remove the jewellery before swimming or showering.
Variations

Faux malachite: pair dark green and mid-green clay, twist into a snake, slice the snake in cross-section, and press the slices into a sheet. The green concentric rings read as the natural mineral banding.
Faux lapis: deep blue clay with tiny gold-leaf flecks pressed into the surface before doming. The gold reads as the pyrite inclusions in natural lapis lazuli.
Cabochons in matching sets: make three of one stone for a set of earrings plus matching ring. The blending step is what takes the time; doming and baking add minimal effort.

items.symptom: Cabochon snaps in half when handled
items.cause: Underbaked; not held at temperature long enough
items.fix: Re-bake at 110°C for a further 15 minutes; verify the oven thermometer reads correctly

items.symptom: Pale clay browns or scorches during baking
items.cause: Oven hotter than the setting (a common fault on domestic ovens)
items.fix: Use an oven thermometer. Lower the dial until the thermometer reads 110°C. If the kitchen oven is not steady, think about a small oven kept just for polymer clay

items.symptom: Granite flecks all blended into a uniform dark grey
items.cause: Folded and rolled too many times
items.fix: Roll a fresh batch; fold and roll four or five times maximum, then check the speckle pattern after each fold

items.symptom: Marble veins muddied into a uniform off-white
items.fix: Twist three or four times maximum; pinch off the clay before the veins blur out
